User story: As a returning player, I want to resume my saved campaign, So that I can continue without reselecting setup options.
Expected behavior: When activeCampaignState exists, the setup screen shows a resume card and restores the selected country, scenario, language, diplomatic partner, and active state.
Test steps: Review setup/resume code; run macOS XCTest suite; verify launch succeeds.
Actual result: Resume path is implemented and covered by persistence tests for active campaign restoration.
Retest result: Passed in full macOS XCTest run.

User story: As a strategy player, I want to choose a scenario before starting, So that the campaign begins in the intended historical timeline.
Expected behavior: Scenario cards show the catalog, selecting one updates selectedScenarioID, and country names/starting state reflect the selected scenario.
Test steps: Review scenario picker and scenario catalog; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Scenario selection is implemented and backed by scenario expansion/customization tests.
Retest result: Passed in full macOS XCTest run.

User story: As a player, I want to choose the app language, So that game content and UI use my preferred language.
Expected behavior: Language picker updates store.selectedLanguage, app language preferences, and game state language; settings explains that restart may be required for full interface refresh.
Test steps: Review setup/settings language flows; run lint and macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Language selection exists in setup and settings, persists through store APIs, and localized string resources are present.
Retest result: Passed in lint and full macOS XCTest run.

Feature name: AI provider setup before campaign start
User story: As a player using AI turns, I want to select and configure the AI provider before starting, So that the first campaign turn can use the provider I chose.
Expected behavior: Setup exposes Apple Foundation Models, OpenRouter Free Router, and Z.AI choices; external providers require API keys before continuing; countries cannot be started until the selected provider is usable.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/CountrySelectionView.swift providerSetupDeck providerCard apiKeyField; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift provider setters; selectors native-provider-* native-provider-continue native-provider-start-warning
Test steps: Review macOS and iOS setup flow; patch missing macOS setup; run lint, full macOS XCTest, and build_and_run verification.
Actual result: Provider setup is now present in macOS setup and country rows are disabled until provider requirements are met.
Bug / UX issue found: Mac setup previously skipped provider setup and could enter campaign with an unusable external provider.
Fix notes: Added providerSetupDeck to macOS setup, provider warning row, and country start gating.
Retest result: Passed lint, full macOS XCTest, and build_and_run verification.

User story: As a player starting a campaign, I want to search for a country, So that I can quickly find the country I want to play.
Expected behavior: Search filters by country name or code, clear resets the query, and an empty state appears when there are no matches.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/CountrySelectionView.swift searchField countryList clearSearch; selectors native-country-search native-country-empty native-clear-country-search
Test steps: Review search/filter code and iOS UI path; update iOS UI test for provider setup; run lint, macOS XCTest suite, and iOS UI smoke test.
Actual result: Search, clear, and empty-state UI are implemented; iOS UI test now reaches search after provider selection.
Bug / UX issue found: iOS UI test flow was stale because provider setup now appears before country search.
Fix notes: Updated PaxHistoriaiOSUITests to select provider and continue before searching.
Retest result: Passed lint, full macOS XCTest, and dedicated iOS Simulator UI smoke test.

User story: As a new player, I want to start as a selected country, So that a playable campaign state is created.
Expected behavior: Choosing a country initializes state for the selected scenario/language/provider, persists the selected country, and requests suggestions when appropriate.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/CountrySelectionView.swift CountryCard choose; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift choose; selectors native-country-option-* native-country-continue
Test steps: Review choose flow and provider gating; run full macOS XCTest and launch verification.
Actual result: Campaign start path creates state; start buttons now respect provider readiness.
Bug / UX issue found: Start controls could be reached while provider configuration was incomplete.
Fix notes: Disabled country start until the provider deck reports usable configuration.
Retest result: Passed full macOS XCTest and build_and_run verification.

User story: As a desktop player, I want a sidebar and toolbar for campaign sections, So that I can move quickly between map, orders, intelligence, economy, library, and settings.
Expected behavior: The macOS shell uses a split navigation layout with sidebar destinations and toolbar shortcuts for orders, advance, and advisor.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeGameView.swift NativeGameShell NativeMacDestination; selectors native-mac-split-shell native-toolbar-orders native-toolbar-advance native-toolbar-advisor
Test steps: Review shell navigation code; run build_and_run verification.
Actual result: macOS navigation destinations and toolbar shortcuts are implemented and app launches successfully.

User story: As a mobile player, I want tabs and compact command buttons, So that I can access core game actions on a small screen.
Expected behavior: The iOS shell exposes Map, Orders, and Intel tabs plus command buttons/drawer for orders, advisor, diplomacy, and advance.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeGameView.swift TabView NativeMapCommandBar CommandConsoleDrawer; selectors native-tab-map native-tab-orders native-tab-intel native-command-*
Test steps: Review iOS shell code and updated iOS UI test selectors; run lint and iOS UI smoke test.
Actual result: Mobile navigation code and selectors are present; source compiles through shared lint/build checks and passes iOS smoke navigation.
Retest result: Passed lint and dedicated iOS Simulator UI smoke test for Map, Orders, Intel, Diplomacy, and Events navigation.

User story: As a first-time player, I want guided contextual help, So that I understand campaign controls before playing.
Expected behavior: On first campaign entry, contextual help pages can be advanced, skipped, or completed; reduced-motion preferences are respected by related visual effects.
Test steps: Review contextual help state and controls; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Help overlay state and controls are implemented with persistent completion behavior.

User story: As a campaign player, I want an overview dashboard, So that I can understand national status, objectives, recent orders, and next decisions.
Expected behavior: Overview renders hero status, metrics, objectives, recent orders, advisor prompts, and map/intelligence summaries with responsive macOS/mobile composition.
Test steps: Review overview screen composition; run build_and_run verification.
Actual result: Overview dashboard is implemented and reachable from app shell.

User story: As a strategist, I want map layers and a legend, So that I can inspect relations, conflict, economy, and fallout at a glance.
Expected behavior: Map supports layer selection, legend rendering, relation/conflict/economic/fallout overlays, and region selection.
Test steps: Review map components and layer model; run lint and build verification.
Actual result: Layer picker, map, and legend code are implemented and compile.

User story: As a strategist, I want to zoom and inspect regions, So that I can focus on local conditions and choose regional actions.
Expected behavior: Map controls adjust zoom/position, selecting a region opens a dossier, and regional actions are disabled when command capacity is insufficient.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeMapComponents.swift RegionDetailsCard regional actions; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeWorldMap.swift; selectors native-region-dossier native-regional-action-*
Test steps: Review zoom, selection, and dossier code; run lint.
Actual result: Region dossier and regional action disable states are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want to advance time by selected intervals, So that planned actions resolve through the AI engine.
Expected behavior: Advance controls offer 1, 3, and 12 month jumps, show progress, call selected provider, validate the generated turn, apply results, and surface provider/validation errors.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeGameView.swift NativeAdvanceMenu NativeFloatingAdvanceButton; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift advance performAdvance; selectors native-advance-*
Test steps: Review advance menu/store flow; run full macOS XCTest and build verification.
Actual result: Advance flow is implemented and tested across provider routing and validation paths.
Retest result: Passed full macOS XCTest and build_and_run verification.

User story: As a player waiting for AI resolution, I want visible turn progress, So that I know the app is working and which provider/model is active.
Expected behavior: During turn generation the overlay shows phase, progress, provider/model summary, and console-style logs; reduce-motion settings avoid decorative animation.
Test steps: Review overlay and store progress updates; run lint/build verification.
Actual result: Progress overlay is implemented and compiles.

User story: As a player after a time jump, I want a turn report, So that I can review what changed before continuing.
Expected behavior: After a successful turn, a report sheet shows summary, metrics, events, and action results; closing clears the report state.
Test steps: Review report sheet and close handling; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Turn report sheet is implemented and report state is cleared through store flow.

User story: As a player who reaches an end state, I want a clear victory or defeat message, So that I understand the campaign outcome and can exit cleanly.
Expected behavior: When victoryStatus is no longer ongoing, a blocking overlay explains the outcome and offers exit to main menu.
Test steps: Review victory status handling and overlay; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Victory/defeat overlay and exit path are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want to write my own order, So that I can plan custom strategic actions.
Expected behavior: The order editor accepts title/details, previews cost/effects, disables add for empty or over-capacity orders, and inserts valid orders into the queue.
Test steps: Review editor validation and store insertion; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Manual order creation and validation paths are implemented.

User story: As a player drafting an order, I want a preview of cost and likely effects, So that I can avoid invalid or wasteful actions.
Expected behavior: Preview shows command cost, remaining capacity, projected effects, and warnings before the order is added.
Test steps: Review preview panel and engine preview code; run lint.
Actual result: Directive preview exists and warning logic compiles.

User story: As a player, I want quick action templates, So that I can add common orders without writing from scratch.
Expected behavior: Templates are grouped by category and create planned orders with localized titles/details and command costs.
Test steps: Review quick action catalog and picker; run full macOS XCTest.
Actual result: Quick action templates are implemented and accepted by store insertion paths.

User story: As a player managing the queue, I want to remove planned orders, So that I can recover command capacity before advancing.
Expected behavior: Deleting an order removes it from plannedActions and refunds command capacity up to the maximum cap.
Test steps: Review delete/refund store logic; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Delete/refund behavior is implemented and covered by backend tests.

User story: As a player, I want AI-suggested orders, So that I can turn advisor recommendations into queued actions quickly.
Expected behavior: Suggested actions can be refreshed from the selected provider, show loading/error/empty states, and accepting a suggestion adds it as a planned action.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeOrdersComponents.swift NativeSuggestedActionsPanel; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift refreshSuggestedActions addSuggestedAction; selectors native-refresh-suggestions native-use-suggestion-*
Test steps: Review suggestions panel/provider/store path; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Suggestion refresh, error, empty, and accept paths are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want to ask the advisor a question, So that I can get contextual strategic guidance.
Expected behavior: Advisor question input disables empty/loading submissions, sends the prompt to the selected provider, persists transcript entries, and shows provider errors without losing the question.
Test steps: Review advisor UI and store error handling; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Advisor ask flow and provider error preservation are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want to message another country, So that I can negotiate or probe diplomatic positions.
Expected behavior: The diplomacy panel selects a partner, sends a message through the selected provider, appends the reply to the thread, and shows provider errors without fabricating a reply.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeIntelComponents.swift NativeDiplomacyPanel; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift sendDiplomaticMessage; selectors native-diplomacy-partner native-diplomacy-message native-send-diplomacy
Test steps: Review diplomacy panel and store flow; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Diplomatic messaging and error handling are implemented.

User story: As a player receiving offers, I want to accept, counter, or reject proposals, So that treaty outcomes affect the campaign state.
Expected behavior: Pending offers show accept/counter/reject actions; accepting/countering updates offer status, relationships, ledger, and timeline; accepted offers appear in the treaty stack.
Test steps: Review offer controls and store methods; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Offer controls, status changes, ledger entries, and treaty stack are implemented.

User story: As a strategist, I want to inspect the diplomatic network, So that I can understand major actors and relationships.
Expected behavior: Network panel summarizes top actors, relationship scores, agendas, and diplomatic posture for the current campaign.
Test steps: Review network panel code; run lint/build verification.
Actual result: Diplomatic network panel is implemented and reachable in intelligence surfaces.

User story: As a player, I want to read campaign events, So that I can understand recent world changes and action consequences.
Expected behavior: Events screen lists timeline events with kind, importance, description, and empty-state messaging when no events exist.
Test steps: Review events panel code; run build verification.
Actual result: Events timeline and empty state are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want economic panels and budget sliders, So that I can track finances and set national priorities.
Expected behavior: Economics panels show player budget, world economic cards, fog-of-war masking, and budget sliders that rebalance to a 100 percent allocation.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeEconomicsComponents.swift PlayerBudgetPanel NativeWorldEconomicsPanel; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift updateBudgetSliders; selectors native-economics-* native-budget-*
Test steps: Review economics panels and budget update logic; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Economic panels and budget balancing logic are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want to adjust difficulty and scenario settings, So that I can tailor the campaign mode and restart scenario-specific state when needed.
Expected behavior: Settings expose difficulty/game mode and scenario selection; scenario changes reinitialize campaign state according to selected scenario rules.
Test steps: Review settings controls and store mutations; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Difficulty/scenario settings are implemented and covered by scenario/state tests.

Feature name: AI provider status and API keys
User story: As a player configuring AI, I want provider status and key fields, So that I know whether the selected AI path can run turns.
Expected behavior: Settings expose provider picker, OpenRouter/Z.AI key fields, status checks, and warning copy that matches the selected provider without implying silent fallback.
Test steps: Review provider settings/status copy; patch stale copy; run lint and full macOS XCTest.
Actual result: Provider settings are implemented and copy now accurately says OpenRouter requires an API key.
Bug / UX issue found: OpenRouter text previously suggested fallback behavior inconsistent with selected-provider correctness.
Fix notes: Updated OpenRouter subtitle/warning copy to describe required API key without implying fallback.
Retest result: Passed lint and full macOS XCTest run.

User story: As a player, I want visible save slots, So that I can switch between campaigns without hidden state management.
Expected behavior: Settings show three save slots with active/empty summaries; switching slots immediately loads that slot or returns to setup when empty.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeSettingsComponents.swift NativeSaveSlotPicker; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore+Persistence.swift switchSlot slotSummary; selectors native-save-slot-picker native-save-slot-summary-*
Test steps: Review persistence APIs and settings surface; add missing UI; run lint, full macOS XCTest, and build verification.
Actual result: Save slot UI is now present in settings and uses existing slot APIs.
Bug / UX issue found: Save-slot backend existed but had no visible settings control for players.
Fix notes: Added NativeSaveSlotPicker and inserted it into settings after the scenario picker.
Retest result: Passed lint, full macOS XCTest, and build_and_run verification.

User story: As a player, I want to import and export campaign data, So that I can back up or move a campaign.
Expected behavior: Library exports campaign JSON with a generated filename, imports validated JSON, rejects oversize/invalid data, and shows success/error messages.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeLibraryComponents.swift NativeLibraryPanel; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeGameView.swift fileImporter fileExporter; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift exportCampaignData importCampaignData; selectors native-library-export native-library-import
Test steps: Review library panel, importer/exporter, and store validation; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Import/export flows and validation are implemented.

User story: As a player, I want local saves to recover from interruption or corruption, So that campaign progress is not lost unnecessarily.
Expected behavior: Persistence writes slot-scoped envelopes/backups, prefers the newest valid save, falls back to backup/legacy data, and records recovery notices.
Test steps: Review persistence implementation and tests; run full macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Slot-scoped persistence, backup fallback, legacy import, and recovery notices are implemented and tested.

User story: As a player selecting an AI provider, I want all AI surfaces to use that provider, So that turns, suggestions, advisor, and diplomacy stay consistent.
Expected behavior: DynamicAIService routes turn generation, suggestions, advisor, and diplomacy through the selected provider; OpenRouter free mode uses the unified turn route and does not silently fall back to Apple.
Test steps: Run focused OpenRouter routing test; fix fixture issue; run full macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Provider routing tests now pass and validate all game AI surfaces through OpenRouter free mode.
Bug / UX issue found: Test fixture generated an invalid linkedActionID when no planned action existed, causing provider-routing test failure.
Fix notes: Updated CapturingOpenRouterGameService fixture to emit null linkedActionID when there is no planned action.
Retest result: Focused routing test and full macOS XCTest passed.

User story: As a player encountering failures, I want clear visible errors, So that I know what failed and what I can fix.
Expected behavior: Provider errors, import errors, suggestion failures, recovery warnings, and validation failures are shown through banners/warnings instead of silent failure.
Test steps: Review error-state UI and store error assignments; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Error banners/warnings are implemented across provider, import, suggestion, and persistence paths.

User story: As a player changing state during AI work, I want stale responses ignored, So that old provider replies cannot corrupt the current campaign.
Expected behavior: State versioning and cancellation invalidate in-flight turn/suggestion/advisor/diplomacy work after reset, import, slot switch, or campaign replacement.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore.swift stateVersion cancelInFlightTurn invalidateInFlightWork; provider task call sites
Test steps: Review async guard implementation and tests; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Stale-response guards and cancellation hooks are implemented and covered by backend/error-path tests.

User story: As a keyboard, VoiceOver, or automation user, I want controls to have labels and stable identifiers, So that the app is navigable and testable.
Expected behavior: Core setup, navigation, orders, advisor, diplomacy, map, import/export, provider, and save-slot controls expose accessibility labels/identifiers; motion-heavy surfaces respect reduce-motion state.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/*.swift accessibilityLabel/accessibilityIdentifier usage; selectors native-*; NativeContextualHelp.swift; TurnTransitionOverlay.swift
Test steps: Review accessibility identifiers/labels in touched and core surfaces; run lint/build verification.
Actual result: Core controls have stable identifiers and labels; new provider/save-slot controls include identifiers. Full manual VoiceOver pass remains outside this automated audit.
Bug / UX issue found: Missing identifiers would have made new provider/save-slot UI harder to test if left unaddressed.
Fix notes: Added identifiers for provider start warning and save-slot picker/summary rows.

Feature name: Native offline behavior and no service worker
User story: As a native app user, I want offline expectations to be clear, So that local saves work while provider-backed AI actions fail visibly without network/API access.
Expected behavior: The active Apple app has no service worker; offline resilience is local persistence/import/export plus visible provider errors when external AI cannot be reached.
Relevant files / functions / selectors: Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/README.md; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeCampaignStore+Persistence.swift; Apple/PaxHistoriaApple/NativeOpenRouterService.swift; no service-worker files in active Apple surface
Test steps: Inspect active Apple surface for service-worker/PWA code; review persistence and provider error paths.
Actual result: No service worker is implemented in the active native product; local persistence and provider error paths cover offline behavior.
Fix notes: No fix needed; documented as not applicable to native Apple app.
Retest result: Reviewed current codebase and final report documents scope.

Feature name: Manual save, reset, and exit to menu
User story: As a player, I want save/reset/exit controls, So that I can preserve progress, clear a campaign, or return to setup intentionally.
Expected behavior: Settings exposes manual save unless ironman mode makes autosave authoritative, reset clears the selected slot/campaign, and exit returns to setup while preserving restorable state.
Test steps: Review settings action controls and persistence methods; run macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Manual save, reset, and exit-to-menu flows are implemented with persistence hooks.

User story: As a player using remote providers, I want rate-limit handling, So that the app behaves predictably under provider throttling or transient failures.
Expected behavior: Remote provider calls use rate limiter/backoff paths, report provider-specific errors, and expose selected-provider status without leaking secrets.
Test steps: Review rate limiter and provider services; run full macOS XCTest suite.
Actual result: Rate limiter and provider error mapping are implemented; live provider tests skip without environment keys.
Retest result: Passed full macOS XCTest; live provider tests skipped because no live API env was configured.

User story: As a maintainer, I want the iOS UI smoke test to follow the real setup flow, So that future UI regressions catch broken onboarding.
Expected behavior: The UI test launches the app, selects an AI provider, continues to country selection, searches for Brazil, and verifies core campaign tabs.
Test steps: Review and patch stale UI test path; run lint, macOS build/test gates, and dedicated iOS Simulator UI smoke test.
Actual result: UI test source matches the provider-first setup flow; dedicated simulator execution passed on PaxHistoria-iPhone-SE (iOS 26.5).
Bug / UX issue found: UI test previously searched countries before completing provider setup.
Fix notes: Inserted provider selection and continue steps before country search.
Retest result: Passed lint, shared macOS gates, and dedicated iOS Simulator UI smoke test on PaxHistoria-iPhone-SE (iOS 26.5).
